the-rise-of-islam.script: The Rise of Islam. You are entering the second part of our journey, which this board also calls the Second Persian Revolution. Here, in the boards ahead, we leave the age of Cyrus and come to the deserts of Arabia, where a new light was about to break upon a world that had sunk into darkness. Before you stands the Ka'bah at Mecca, the ancient House of God, with the minarets of a great mosque rising behind it. This is the setting for the story of how Islam arose, and of the remarkable place that Persia and its people would come to hold within it. Let us begin in an age of darkness.

an-age-of-darkness.script: An Age of Darkness. Before you a dark sea breaks against the shore under a deep blue night, a fitting image for a world in decay. As the Holy Quran says, in chapter thirty, verse forty-two: "Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what men's hands have wrought, that He may make them taste the fruit of some of their doings, so that they may turn back from evil." By the seventh century, Persia was no longer the empire of Cyrus the Great. The Achaemenid Empire had lasted for over two centuries, broadly keeping its tolerant Zoroastrian spirit. But in around three hundred and thirty BCE it was destroyed by Alexander the Great, and though three Persian empires followed, Zoroastrianism itself changed: the one supreme God was replaced by multiple spirits, fire and sun-worship grew, and the Zoroastrian priestly class began to persecute minorities. Yet the Persians were not alone in their corruption. For centuries they were locked in conflict with the Romans, whose rulers brutalised non-orthodox Christians and impoverished their peoples with taxation, so that deep corruption bred resentment from within. Both the Persians and the Romans held client Arab states beneath them. The Persians ruled Yemen as a province and controlled the Lakhmids of the Levant; and in around six hundred CE, the Sasanian king Khoswrow the Second murdered al-Nu'man the Third, the Arab king of the Lakhmids, causing uproar among the Arab tribes. The world of Arabia itself was little different: inter-tribal warfare raged, baby girls were sometimes killed at birth, and slavery was rife and brutal. And yet it was in this very world that something new emerged. In the words of Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad: "Have you any notion what was the strange event that occurred in the desert country of Arabia when hundreds of thousands of the dead were revived within a brief period and those who had been misguided through generations put on Divine colour, and those who were blind obtained sight, and those who had been dumb began to speak of the understanding of the Divine, and the world underwent a revolution which had never been seen or heard of before? It was the supplications during dark nights of one who had lost himself in God which raised a clamour in the world, and manifested such wonders as appeared impossible in the case of that unlearned helpless one. Send down Thy blessings and peace, O Allah, on him and his people according to the amount of pain and anguish he felt for his Ummah, and pour down upon him the lights of Thy mercy forever."

verses.text: Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what men's hands have wrought, that He may make them taste the fruit of some of their doings, so that they may turn back from evil.

Have you any notion what was the strange event that occurred in the desert country of Arabia when hundreds of thousands of the dead were revived within a brief period and those who had been misguided through generations put on Divine colour, and those who were blind obtained sight, and those who had been dumb began to speak of the understanding of the Divine, and the world underwent a revolution which had never been seen or heard of before? It was the supplications during dark nights of one who had lost himself in God which raised a clamour in the world, and manifested such wonders as appeared impossible in the case of that unlearned helpless one. Send down Thy blessings and peace, O Allah, on him and his people according to the amount of pain and anguish he felt for his Ummah, and pour down upon him the lights of Thy mercy forever.

a-new-dawn.script: A New Dawn. Before you a glowing sunrise rises over the sea, the dawn of a new age. The Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, was born in Mecca in five hundred and seventy CE, into a noble family, but he lost his father, mother and grandfather at a young age. Overseen by his uncle, he became a young man renowned for his piety and trustworthiness. At the age of twenty-five he married Khadija, a forty-year-old merchant-woman, and he spent much of his time in the Cave of Hira, praying for days on end to God Almighty. In six hundred and ten CE, he began to receive revelations from Allah. This Quranic revelation continued for some twenty-three lunar years until his demise in six hundred and thirty-two CE, and the core of the message was simple: "There is no God but Allah, and Muhammad is His Messenger." It came at a time when the unity of God had been lost, giving way to Christian Trinitarianism and to various forms of polytheism, and Islam restored pure unity to the world. As the Holy Quran declares, in chapter two, verse two hundred and fifty-six: "Allah is He save Whom none is worthy of worship, the Ever-Living, the Self-Subsisting and All-Sustaining. Slumber seizes Him not, nor sleep. To Him belongs whatsoever is in the heavens and whatsoever is in the earth." These Islamic beliefs were beautifully set out one day in the presence of the Prophet himself, in a narration related by Umar ibn Khattab, in the collection of Sahih Muslim: "We were sitting one day with the Holy Prophet when a man appeared among us whose clothes were of an intense whiteness, whose hair was very black, who bore no mark of travel and who was not known to any of us. He sat down in front of the Holy Prophet, their knees touching, and placing his hands on his thighs he said: Muhammad, tell me about Islam. The Holy Prophet said: Islam is that you should bear witness that there is no one worthy of worship save Allah alone and that Muhammad is His Messenger, and that you should observe Prayer, pay the Zakat, observe the fast during Ramadhan, and perform the Pilgrimage to the House if you can afford the journey thither. The man said: That is right. We were surprised that he inquired and also confirmed the correctness of the answer. He then said: Tell me about faith. The Holy Prophet said: That you should believe in Allah, His Angels, His Books, His Messengers, the Last Day, and that you should believe that He determines the measure of good and evil. The man said: That is right. Now tell me about the due performance of obligations. The Holy Prophet said: That you should worship Allah as if you are beholding Him, and if not then in the consciousness that He is watching you. Then the man departed, and I remained a while. The Holy Prophet said to me: Umar, do you know who the questioner was? I said: Allah and His Messenger know best. He said: It was Gabriel who came to instruct you in your faith."

verses.text: There is no God but Allah, and Muhammad is His Messenger

Allah is He save Whom none is worthy of worship, the Ever-Living, the Self-Subsisting and All-Sustaining. Slumber seizes Him not, nor sleep. To Him belongs whatsoever is in the heavens and whatsoever is in the earth...

We were sitting one day with the Holy Prophet when a man appeared among us whose clothes were of an intense whiteness, whose hair was very black, who bore no mark of travel and who was not known to any of us. He sat down in front of the Holy Prophet, their knees touching, and placing his hands on his thighs he said: Muhammad, tell me about Islam. The Holy Prophet said: Islam is that you should bear witness that there is no one worthy of worship save Allah alone and that Muhammad is His Messenger, and that you should observe Prayer, pay the Zakat, observe the fast during Ramadhan, and perform the Pilgrimage to the House if you can afford the journey thither. The man said: That is right. We were surprised that he inquired and also confirmed the correctness of the answer. He then said: Tell me about faith. The Holy Prophet said: That you should believe in Allah, His Angels, His Books, His Messengers, the Last Day, and that you should believe that He determines the measure of good and evil. The man said: That is right. Now tell me about the due performance of obligations. The Holy Prophet said: That you should worship Allah as if you are beholding Him, and if not then in the consciousness that He is watching you. Then the man departed, and I remained a while. The Holy Prophet said to me: Umar, do you know who the questioner was? I said: Allah and His Messenger know best. He said: It was Gabriel who came to instruct you in your faith.

the-prophecy-of-rome.script: The Prophecy of Rome. Before you is the weathered stone bust of a Roman emperor. In these early years the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, and the first believers were heavily persecuted for their belief in Allah, the one true God; many were tortured, and many martyred. For over a decade the persecution intensified, until in six hundred and twenty-two CE the Arab tribes united to murder the Holy Prophet, but God informed him of the plot. He escaped from Mecca to Medina, over two hundred miles away. Yet the persecution did not end, and eventually a state of war was thrust upon the Muslims. During this same period, Khoswrow the Second, the Persian emperor, led a stunning military campaign against the Roman emperor Heraclius. The Romans lost Damascus in six hundred and thirteen CE, and Jerusalem, along with the True Cross of Christ, in six hundred and fourteen CE. Rome seemed finished. But in six hundred and sixteen CE came a remarkable prophecy, in the Holy Quran, chapter thirty, verses three to five: "The Romans have been defeated, In the land nearby, and they, after their defeat, will be victorious. In a few years, Allah's is the command before and after that, and on that day the believers will rejoice." This foretold three things at once: that the Romans would be victorious over the Persians again; that the victory would come, in the Arabic word bid'un, within three to nine years; and that the Muslims would be especially glad on that day. In six hundred and twenty-four CE, a Meccan army of strong, seasoned warriors was sent out against the Prophet and his followers, and in the Valley of Badr the great battle would take place. The disparity was stark. The Meccans fielded one thousand men, seven hundred camels and one hundred horses; the Muslims, only three hundred and thirteen men, seventy camels and two horses. The night before, the Prophet was informed of victory, and so it was. And incredibly, it was on this very day that the Muslims learned that the Romans had indeed defeated the Persians; for Heraclius' counter-offensive campaign, beginning in six hundred and twenty-two, had reached great success in six hundred and twenty-four, defeating three Persian armies in succession. Thus all three arms of the prophecy were fulfilled: the Romans were victorious; the victory came within nine years; and the Muslims too were glad, victorious both in battle and in prophecy.

verses.text: The Romans have been defeated, In the land nearby, and they, after their defeat, will be victorious. In a few years—Allah's is the command before and after that—and on that day the believers will rejoice.

the-persian-companion.script: The Persian Companion. Palm trees rise before a mosque here, a fitting image for Salman Al-Farsi, Salman the Persian. Born in Isfahan, in modern-day Iran, Salman was raised as a Magian firekeeper, that is, a Zoroastrian. He once came across a Christian church, and was so impressed by their worship that, upon telling his father of his desire to convert, he was shackled in the house. After escaping to Syria, he travelled the lands, searching for spiritual teachers who adhered to true righteousness. But on his death bed, his last teacher advised that there were none like him left. He did, however, foretell that a new Prophet was coming to the Arabs, and gave Salman three marks of his Prophethood to test him with, saying that he would come to a land between two bare tracts, populated by palm trees. Salman now had his next destination. He found his way to Arabia, but was betrayed and sold into slavery. Eventually he reached Medina, a land of palm trees, and waited. One day, he heard that a man claiming to be a Prophet was coming to Medina. He rushed to see if he fulfilled the three marks: distributing but not accepting charity, eating of a gift, and bearing a particular mark on his back. Upon testing him, he discovered that the Prophet was true; and from then on, Salman would never leave the Prophet in a time of need. Though he had begun his life as a Magian, Salman played a pivotal role in protecting his Muslim brethren from the idolaters. As the Holy Quran teaches, in chapter twenty-two, verse eighteen: "As to those who believe, and the Jews, and the Sabians, and the Christians, and the Magians and the idolaters, verily, Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection; surely, Allah is Witness over all things." When the Muslims were being attacked by a huge army, Salman used his knowledge of non-Arab warfare to suggest digging a protective trench on the exposed side of Medina. This approach, never seen before by the Arabs, worked, and the Muslims were victorious against the idolaters once more. And when two groups of the companions argued as to whether Salman was of the Emigrants, who left Mecca, or of the Helpers, who were from Medina, the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, intervened, saying: "Salman is from among us, that is, the People of the Prophet's Household."

verses.text: As to those who believe, and the Jews, and the Sabians, and the Christians, and the Magians and the idolaters, verily, Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection; surely, Allah is Witness over all things.
Salmān is from among us, that is, the People of the Prophet's Household.

faith-in-the-pleiades.script: Faith in the Pleiades. This board records a single, luminous moment, narrated by Abu Huraira, in the collection of Bukhari. He relates: "One Day we were sitting with the Holy Prophet when Surah Jumu'ah was revealed. I asked the Holy Prophet, 'Who are the people to whom the words And among others from among them who have not yet joined them, refer?' Salman the Persian was sitting among us. Upon my repeatedly asking him the same question, the Prophet put his hand on Salman and said, 'If Faith were to go up to the Pleiades, a man from among these would surely find it.'" It is a promise that, as this exhibition unfolds, points across the centuries to a reformer of Persian descent.

verses.text: One Day we were sitting with the Holy Prophet when Surah Jumu'ah was revealed. I asked the Holy Prophet, "Who are the people to whom the words "And among others from among them who have not yet joined them", refer? Salman the Persian was sitting among us. Upon my repeatedly asking him the same question, the Prophet put his hand on Salman and said, "If Faith were to go up to the Pleiades, a man from among these would surely find it."

persian-downfall.script: Persian Downfall. Before you, a row of carved Persian heads gazes out from Persepolis. The Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, was commissioned to pronounce his Prophethood to the world, and so he wrote letters to both Heraclius and Khoswrow the Second, inviting them to Islam. Heraclius treated both the message and the emissary who brought it with great respect. Khoswrow, however, was incensed when he read the letter, and tore it to pieces, saying: "He is my servant and he addresses me in this way!" When the Prophet heard how the message of Allah had been treated, he proclaimed: "Now these people shall themselves be shattered to pieces." His letter to Persia had been a message of peace. It read: "I write this letter in the name of Allah, the Gracious, who grants the best reward of our deeds. This letter is from Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah addressed to the Ruler of Persia. Peace be on him who accepts the guidance and believes in Allah and His Messenger and bears witness that there is none worthy of worship except God, He has no partner; And also bears witness that Muhammad is His Servant and Messenger. O Ruler of Persia! I invite you to the call of Allah, as I am the messenger of Allah to the whole of mankind; so that I may warn every living person and so that the judgement of God may overtake the disbelievers. O Ruler of Persia! Accept Islam, for this is the only way to your salvation. However, if you turn away, then remember that the sin of your Zoroastrian people shall also be on your shoulders." But Khoswrow answered with an assassination. He ordered the Governor of Yemen to send two men to arrest the Holy Prophet: he was to give allegiance to the Persian Emperor, or face death. When the emissaries reached the Prophet and gave their demands, the Holy Prophet asked them to stay the night. In the morning, he told them: "Tell your master that my Lord, the God of power, has killed his master last night." When the emissaries returned to Yemen, they told the Governor the prophecy. And within days they received word: Khoswrow the Second had been murdered by his own son, and the murder had taken place on the very night the prophecy was revealed. Though neither Heraclius nor Khoswrow accepted Islam, one gave respect to the message while the other tore it to pieces. And for this reason, the Prophet said: "The Persian Kingdom shall be destroyed at once, but God shall grant some respite to the Roman Empire." And so it was.

verses.text: He is my servant and he addresses me in this way!
Now these people shall themselves be shattered to pieces.

verses.ref: Letter of the Prophet Muhammad to the Ruler of Persia
verses.text: I write this letter in the name of Allāh, the Gracious, who grants the best reward of our deeds. This letter is from Muḥammad, the Messenger of Allāh addressed to the Ruler of Persia. Peace be on him who accepts the guidance and believes in Allāh and His Messenger and bears witness that there is none worthy of worship except God, He has no partner; And also bears witness that Muḥammad is His Servant and Messenger. O Ruler of Persia! I invite you to the call of Allāh, as I am the messenger of Allāh to the whole of mankind; so that I may warn every living person and so that the judgement of God may overtake the disbelievers. O Ruler of Persia! Accept Islām, for this is the only way to your salvation. However, if you turn away, then remember that the sin of your Zoroastrian people shall also be on your shoulders.
Tell your master that my Lord, the God of power, has killed his master last night.
The Persian Kingdom shall be destroyed at once, but God shall grant some respite to the Roman Empire.
